skill.name: wireshark-network-traffic-analysis
skill.description: Guides authorized Wireshark packet capture, filtering, stream analysis, and security-focused PCAP review for troubleshooting and investigations.
skill.summary: Analyze authorized network traffic and PCAP files with Wireshark workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many command, C2, weak crypto, IP address, and network patterns, but review shows they are mostly Wireshark filter examples and defensive analysis terminology in SKILL.md. The real risk is that the skill guides live packet capture, stream reconstruction, credential inspection, and TLS key handling, which can expose sensitive traffic if used without authorization.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Capture Boundary Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run live packet capture with interface permissions and administrator or root access. This is legitimate for Wireshark work, but it can collect traffic from users who did not consent if used outside an authorized environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly describe live capture requirements and warn about authorized traffic and privacy handling. The risk is contextual misuse, not confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Traffic And Key Material Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches users to search packet contents for passwords, follow streams, inspect login requests, and configure TLS decryption with keys or pre-master secrets. These are valid incident response techniques, but they require strict handling controls for credentials and private key material.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly reference password searches, stream reconstruction, login analysis, and private key or secret capture. The surrounding purpose is defensive analysis, so medium risk is appropriate.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown fenced blocks and Wireshark menu steps, not executable code. No evidence found of scripts, shell execution, dynamic command construction, or user input passed to a command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are Markdown examples and static Wireshark filter syntax. They do not invoke a shell or execute code.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Findings Are Defensive Context
low_findings.description: The C2 and Cobalt Strike related signals appear in a malware traffic detection example. The text describes identifying beaconing and suspicious destinations, not creating or operating command and control infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section is labeled as malware C2 detection and lists observation steps. No evidence found of payload creation, persistence, evasion, or offensive C2 instructions.

low_findings.title: Protocol And Address Keyword Findings Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded private IP addresses, HTTP references, email protocol names, and SSL or TLS terms are used as Wireshark filters or protocol labels. They do not indicate network exfiltration, email sending, or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use RFC1918 addresses and protocol filter names inside Wireshark guidance. No code or runtime network request is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Packet Inspection Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes workflows for locating credentials, viewing reconstructed streams, and inspecting login traffic. This can expose secrets in packet captures and should be limited to authorized investigations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly targets password strings and login traffic. It is dual-use but presented for troubleshooting and incident response.

dangerous_patterns.title: TLS Decryption Key Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill advises using server private keys or browser pre-master secrets for TLS analysis. Mishandling these materials could compromise protected communications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line references private keys and pre-master secrets directly. The guidance is legitimate for authorized decryption but carries clear sensitivity.

content.value_statement: Network incidents and performance issues are hard to diagnose from logs alone. This skill provides structured Wireshark workflows for authorized captures, PCAP review, filters, streams, and findings documentation.
actual_capabilities: Guides live packet capture setup and basic capture controls in Wireshark.
Provides capture and display filter examples for hosts, ports, protocols, flags, and content.
Explains how to follow TCP, UDP, HTTP, and TLS streams for conversation review.
Uses Wireshark statistics views for conversations, endpoints, flow graphs, and I/O graphs.
Supports defensive checks for scans, ARP spoofing, suspicious DNS, downloads, and plaintext credentials.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for capture problems, filter syntax, performance, and TLS decryption limits.
limitations: It requires Wireshark and appropriate packet capture permissions on the target system.
It does not automate packet parsing or replace expert interpretation of network evidence.
Encrypted traffic contents are not visible unless authorized keys or secrets are available.
Large captures can be slow and may require filtering before detailed review.

use_cases.title: Investigate Suspicious Traffic
use_cases.description: Review PCAP files for unusual DNS queries, beaconing patterns, unexpected ports, and possible plaintext credential exposure.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Application Latency
use_cases.description: Use conversations, I/O graphs, retransmission filters, and flow graphs to isolate packet loss or response delays.

use_cases.title: Teach Protocol Analysis
use_cases.description: Walk learners through Wireshark filters, stream reconstruction, protocol hierarchy, and packet-level evidence collection.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Basic PCAP Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a provided capture file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me review this PCAP in Wireshark. Start with protocol hierarchy, endpoints, conversations, and the first filters I should apply.

prompt_templates.title: Build Troubleshooting Filters
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Wireshark display filters for slow web traffic involving one client and one server. Include retransmissions, resets, DNS errors, and high-latency indicators.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Suspicious Activity
prompt_templates.scenario: Incident response review of suspicious network behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through a defensive Wireshark analysis for possible malware traffic. Focus on DNS patterns, beacon timing, unusual ports, and evidence notes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare An Investigation Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced evidence summary after packet analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my Wireshark findings into an incident report with scope, filters used, key conversations, timeline, indicators, limitations, and recommended next steps.

output_examples.input: Analyze a capture for slow access to an internal web application.
output: Start with Conversations and I/O Graphs to identify the client, server, and traffic spikes.
Apply retransmission, duplicate ACK, reset, DNS error, and server-specific filters.
Summarize the likely cause, supporting packets, and any capture limitations.

output_examples.input: Review a PCAP for possible credential exposure.
output: Filter for HTTP requests and login-related paths, then follow relevant streams.
Record whether credentials appear in cleartext and identify affected hosts.
Recommend credential rotation and confirm that the capture is stored securely.

output_examples.input: Investigate possible command and control traffic.
output: Check DNS query frequency, random-looking domains, repeated destination connections, and unusual ports.
Compare timing intervals and byte counts across conversations.
Document indicators without attempting to contact or operate suspicious infrastructure.

best_practices: Capture only traffic you are authorized to inspect and minimize collection with capture filters.
Protect PCAP files because they can contain credentials, tokens, private data, and internal topology.
Document filters, timestamps, assumptions, and limitations so findings can be reproduced.
anti_patterns: Capturing broad network traffic without written authorization or a defined scope.
Sharing PCAP files through insecure channels or leaving them on unmanaged devices.
Treating one packet or one alert as proof without validating the full conversation context.

faq.question: Does this skill require Wireshark?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflows are written for Wireshark and assume access to its capture, filter, stream, and statistics features.

faq.question: Can it analyze live traffic?
faq.answer: Yes, but only when you have authorization and the needed interface permissions. Offline PCAP review is often safer.

faq.question: Can it decrypt TLS traffic?
faq.answer: Only in authorized cases where valid keys or secrets are available. Many modern sessions cannot be decrypted passively.

faq.question: Is this useful for incident response?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports defensive review of suspicious DNS, beaconing patterns, downloads, streams, and exposed credentials.

faq.question: Does it send network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found of runtime network requests. The skill provides Wireshark analysis instructions and filter examples.

faq.question: How should sensitive captures be handled?
faq.answer: Store captures securely, limit access, follow privacy policies, and remove or redact data before sharing when possible.
